I thought it'd be useful to start by briefly recapping, uh, what Trans power does and where we fit into the energy system. We are a state owned enterprise that owns and operates New Zealand's high voltage electricity transmission Network, the National Bridge. We are also contracted by the electricity authority to operate the wholesale electricity market through which generators sell energy to retailers.

In this role as a system operator, we want to ensure that the amount of electricity generated matches how much Kiwis need or want to use. To do this, we manage system security and real time all day, every day of the year. Trans power does not generate or retail electricity, nor do we set the prices that consumers pay.

Our revenue is set in a five year period by the Commerce Commission, following a robust process to ensure our investments give consumers good value for money while delivering the reliability, that reliability that Kiwis expect. We recover our revenue from lines, companies, generators, and directly connected industrial users using a method called the TPM set by the electricity authority.

Ultimately, our revenue charges through to consumers with transmission charges making up about 8% of the average residential bill. We are also subject to New Zealand Stock Exchange listing rules with $650 million enlisted debt, which means that we have company reporting and debt listing obligations as well.

Trans power's core purpose is to empower the energy future of New Zealand. Our people are dedicated to keeping the lights on for New Zealanders and ensuring that there is a safe and reliable electricity system that enables our economy. It is something we are deeply passionate about. Looking back on the last year in terms of overall performance, we met all but one of our targets across safety, sustainability, availability of the system, asset health and financial metrics.

We missed one technical measure, a decision we made so that we could complete critical work without impacting supply to consumers. In addition to achieving our performance measures, there are a number of notable highlights I would like to mention, including improvements to our customer connection pipeline, some excellent innovation by our HVDC team, the positive impact of our security of supply reporting and our world leading approach to managing space weather risk.

When someone wants to com, uh, connect to the grid, they must make an application to trans power. We have seen a significant increase in these requests from about three to five requests a year, a few years ago, to now receiving more than 100 in a year. To address this increase in volume, we form dedicated specialized teams who take a more proactive approach.

This has enabled us to focus on those who are ready to proceed. We are now going 80% faster than we were 12 months ago. Last year, we commissioned 300 megawatts of new generation and capacity upgrades about the same amount of power needed for Wellington City at peak times. The HVDC link is a critical part of New Zealand's electricity infrastructure running from Benmore on the south island under the Cook Strait and onto Haywoods just north of Wellington.

We completed a midlife refurbishment to extend the life of specific critical components of this HVDC link. It was a very rare piece of work globally and something that there was no manual for. This challenging and complex work delivered significant value to New Zealand. It costs $63 million, but it has enabled us to defer over a billion dollars for the next 20 years.

As a system operator, our analysis and advice to industry on security of supply risks is critical to keeping the lights on. This year, our reporting gave an early and clear picture to industry during the drier start to a year in history, which prompted the industry to secure more fuel, gas, and coal for electricity much earlier in the year.

As a result, we did not see the significant spice, um, spike in prices and wholesale prices that we saw in 2024. Space weather has the potential to significantly impact and damage the grid leading to lengthy power outages. Transfer has been working for more than a decade to grow our expertise, including investing in university led research.

We have developed a national response plan in conjunction with NEMA and are now supporting the UK with their efforts. As I look to 2026 and beyond, the work that we did last year has set us up well for that and the years to come work on the customer connection pipeline continues as an area of focus.

We have 970 megawatts of new generation booked for commissioning about three times that of last year. We will also complete projects to connect new demand or load. Both aspects are critical for ensuring security of supply and this country's economic growth. In my opening remarks, I referred to the TPM, the regulated method by which we charge our revenue to lines, companies, generators, and directly connected industrial users.

The TPM is highly technical and complex. Recently, we commenced an operational review with the aim to address the TPMS complexity and make it simpler and more predictable. Our aim is to increase certainty and clarity, both for existing investors and those who are looking to invest in new projects.

We will also explore ways to address customer concerns on the cost of recovery for network investment in low population areas situated some distance from the core transmission backbone. In July, we entered a new five year regulatory control period known as RCP four.

RCP four includes intensified work on the national grid to replace or upgrade aging assets. Much of our grid was built during the 1950s and seventies and now requires an increasing level of replacement and refurbishment work. That work as well as investment in new infrastructure is essential to ensuring, ensuring KIWIS continue to have a safe and reliable national grid.

It is also an an important foundation for future enhancements to support electrification and economic growth. Long-term thinking to ensure that the grid remains fit for purpose for years to come is vital.
